section.title: General solid regions
section.p: lived inside boxes. Boxes are a good first home because their bounds do not move: a\le x\le b,\qquad c\le y\le d,\qquad e\le z\le f. A general solid is different. One slice may be short, another tall, another empty. The limits have to tell that story.

subsection.title: Describing a solid by inequalities
subsection.p: Picture a small tent in the corner of a coordinate room. Its floor lies in the first quadrant of the xy-plane, and its sloping roof is the plane z=6-x-y. The tent touches the coordinate planes x=0,\qquad y=0,\qquad z=0. The solid inside the tent is E=\{(x,y,z):x\ge0,\ y\ge0,\ z\ge0,\ x+y+z\le6\}.

The same solid can be described by nested inequalities. Choose x first: 0\le x\le 6. Once x is fixed, y can run from 0 to 6-x: 0\le y\le 6-x. Once x and y are fixed, z runs from the floor to the roof: 0\le z\le 6-x-y. So \boxed{E=\left\{(x,y,z):0\le x\le6,\ 0\le y\le 6-x,\ 0\le z\le 6-x-y\right\}.}

figure.caption: The solid x,y,z\ge0,\ x+y+z\le6 is a tetrahedron. (Interactive: drag to rotate, scroll to zoom.)

subsection.p:
Its volume is \iiint_E 1\,dV. Using the bounds above,
\operatorname{Volume}(E) \amp= \int_0^6\int_0^{6-x}\int_0^{6-x-y} 1\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^6\int_0^{6-x} (6-x-y)\,dy\,dx.
Compute the y-integral:
\int_0^{6-x} (6-x-y)\,dy \amp= \left[(6-x)y-\frac12y^2\right]_0^{6-x} \amp= \frac12(6-x)^2.
Therefore
\operatorname{Volume}(E) \amp= \int_0^6 \frac12(6-x)^2\,dx \amp= \frac12\left[-\frac{(6-x)^3}{3}\right]_0^6 \amp= 36.
So \boxed{\operatorname{Volume}(E)=36.}
The bounds were not guessed. They came from slicing the solid.

subsection.title: Slicing perpendicular to coordinate axes
subsection.p: The tent example used vertical slices. For each fixed point (x,y) in the shadow on the xy-plane, the z-coordinate ran from the bottom surface to the top surface.
That pattern is common enough to name.

For a z-simple solid, the triple integral is \boxed{\iiint_E f(x,y,z)\,dV = \iint_D \left(\int_{g_1(x,y)}^{g_2(x,y)} f(x,y,z)\,dz\right)dA.} After that, the double integral over D is handled by the methods of .
If D is Type I, D=\{(x,y):a\le x\le b,\ \phi_1(x)\le y\le \phi_2(x)\}, then \boxed{\iiint_E f\,dV = \int_a^b \int_{\phi_1(x)}^{\phi_2(x)} \int_{g_1(x,y)}^{g_2(x,y)} f(x,y,z)\,dz\,dy\,dx.}

theorem.title: Triple integral over a z-simple solid

statement.p: Suppose E=\left\{(x,y,z):(x,y)\in D,\ g_1(x,y)\le z\le g_2(x,y)\right\}, where g_1 and g_2 are continuous, and suppose f is continuous on E. Then \boxed{\iiint_E f(x,y,z)\,dV = \iint_D \int_{g_1(x,y)}^{g_2(x,y)} f(x,y,z)\,dz\,dA.}

proof.title: Idea of the proof
proof.p: For each fixed (x,y) in the shadow D, the vertical line through (x,y) cuts the solid in the interval g_1(x,y)\le z\le g_2(x,y). The inner integral adds f along that vertical slice. The outer double integral adds all the vertical slices over the shadow D.
This is the same slicing idea as Fubini's theorem, except now the slice endpoints depend on position.

subsection.p: The word z-simple matters. A vertical line must hit the solid in one interval. If it hits the solid in two separated pieces, one pair of z-limits cannot describe the solid without filling in empty space.

example.title: Why one slice must be one interval

statement.p: Let E=\bigl([0,1]\times[0,1]\times[0,1]\bigr)\cup\bigl([0,1]\times[0,1]\times[2,3]\bigr). This solid is two square shelves with a gap between them.

For each point (x,y) in the unit square, the vertical line meets the solid in 0\le z\le1 and also in 2\le z\le3. It does not meet the gap 1\lt z\lt2. If we wrote 0\le z\le3, we would describe a full box of volume 3, not two shelves of total volume 2.

subsection.p: There are also x-simple and y-simple solids.

An x-simple solid has the form E=\left\{(x,y,z):(y,z)\in D_{yz},\ h_1(y,z)\le x\le h_2(y,z)\right\}. A y-simple solid has the form E=\left\{(x,y,z):(x,z)\in D_{xz},\ k_1(x,z)\le y\le k_2(x,z)\right\}. The same solid may be simple in more than one direction. The best direction is the one that makes the bounds easiest.

subsection.title: Regions bounded by surfaces
subsection.p: Many solids are described as the region between two surfaces above a plane region.

example.title: A layer between two slanted surfaces

statement.p:
A layer of insulation fills the region above the rectangle 0\le x\le2,\qquad 0\le y\le1. Its lower surface is z=1+x, and its upper surface is z=5-y. Since 1+x\le 5-y on this rectangle, the solid is E=\left\{(x,y,z):0\le x\le2,\ 0\le y\le1,\ 1+x\le z\le 5-y\right\}. Its volume is
\operatorname{Volume}(E) \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1\int_{1+x}^{5-y}1\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1 \bigl((5-y)-(1+x)\bigr)\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1 (4-x-y)\,dy\,dx.
Now compute:
\int_0^1(4-x-y)\,dy \amp= 4-x-\frac12 \amp= \frac72-x.
Thus
\operatorname{Volume}(E) \amp= \int_0^2 \left(\frac72-x\right)\,dx \amp= 7-2 \amp= 5.
So \boxed{\operatorname{Volume}(E)=5.}

subsection.p: The inner integral with respect to z simply subtracts top minus bottom: (5-y)-(1+x). So for volume between two graphs, \boxed{\iiint_E 1\,dV = \iint_D \bigl(\text{top}-\text{bottom}\bigr)\,dA.} This is exactly the double-integral volume idea from . Triple integrals did not erase that idea; they placed it inside a larger one.

statement.p:
Use the same solid 0\le x\le2,\qquad 0\le y\le1,\qquad 1+x\le z\le 5-y, but suppose the density is \rho(x,y,z)=z. Then
M \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1\int_{1+x}^{5-y} z\,dz\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^1 \left[\frac12 z^2\right]_{1+x}^{5-y}\,dy\,dx \amp= \frac12 \int_0^2\int_0^1 \left((5-y)^2-(1+x)^2\right)\,dy\,dx.
Compute the two pieces: \int_0^1(5-y)^2\,dy = \left[-\frac{(5-y)^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \frac{125-64}{3} = \frac{61}{3}, and \int_0^2(1+x)^2\,dx = \left[\frac{(1+x)^3}{3}\right]_0^2 = \frac{27-1}{3} = \frac{26}{3}. Since the first term is integrated over x\in[0,2] and the second over y\in[0,1],
M \amp= \frac12 \left(2\cdot\frac{61}{3} - 1\cdot\frac{26}{3}\right) \amp= \frac12\cdot\frac{96}{3} \amp= 16.
So \boxed{M=16.}

subsection.p: The bounds describe the solid. The integrand describes what is spread through it.

subsection.title: Changing the order of integration in space
subsection.p: Changing the order in two dimensions meant redrawing a region in the plane. Changing the order in three dimensions means redrawing a solid, or at least redrawing its shadow on a coordinate plane.

Return to the tetrahedron E=\{(x,y,z):x\ge0,\ y\ge0,\ z\ge0,\ x+y+z\le6\}. One order was \boxed{\iiint_E f\,dV = \int_0^6\int_0^{6-x}\int_0^{6-x-y} f(x,y,z)\,dz\,dy\,dx.} This order uses vertical z-slices. The shadow lies in the xy-plane: 0\le x\le6,\qquad 0\le y\le6-x.

Now choose x as the innermost variable. Then the shadow lies in the yz-plane. For fixed y and z, x runs from the plane x=0 to the slanted plane x=6-y-z. The yz-shadow is the triangle 0\le y\le6,\qquad 0\le z\le6-y. So the same integral can be written as \boxed{\iiint_E f\,dV = \int_0^6\int_0^{6-y}\int_0^{6-y-z} f(x,y,z)\,dx\,dz\,dy.}

A third order is \boxed{\iiint_E f\,dV = \int_0^6\int_0^{6-z}\int_0^{6-z-x} f(x,y,z)\,dy\,dx\,dz.} All three describe the same solid. The only difference is which coordinate direction is used for the innermost slice.

figure.caption: Changing the innermost variable changes the coordinate shadow we must draw.

subsection.p: Sometimes changing order is not only possible; it is the difference between a hard integral and an easy one.

example.title: Changing order to avoid a hard antiderivative

statement.p: Consider I=\int_0^1\int_0^1\int_y^1 e^{x^2}\,dx\,dz\,dy. The inner integral \int_y^1 e^{x^2}\,dx has no elementary antiderivative.
The solid did not change. The grouping of its slices changed.

subsection.p: The habit from still works: \boxed{\text{Draw the shadow before writing the bounds.}} For a triple integral, choose the innermost variable first. Then draw the shadow on the plane of the two outer variables.

subsection.title: A compact guide to setting up bounds
subsection.p: When setting up a triple integral, use the following routine.

li.p: Look along that coordinate direction and describe the first and last surfaces hit.
Project the solid onto the plane of the two remaining variables.
Describe that shadow using the two-dimensional methods from .
Write the integral from inside to outside.

subsection.p: For example, if z goes inside, use \int\int\int_{\text{bottom}}^{\text{top}} f(x,y,z)\,dz\,dA. If the xy-shadow is Type I, this becomes \int_a^b \int_{\phi_1(x)}^{\phi_2(x)} \int_{g_1(x,y)}^{g_2(x,y)} f(x,y,z)\,dz\,dy\,dx.
If x goes inside, use \int\int\int_{\text{left}}^{\text{right}} f(x,y,z)\,dx\,dA_{yz}. If y goes inside, use \int\int\int_{\text{back}}^{\text{front}} f(x,y,z)\,dy\,dA_{xz}.
The names left, right, front, and back depend on the picture. The mathematics is the inequality.

Rectangular coordinates are good for boxes, wedges, slabs, and planes. But they are not always the natural language of space. A can, a pipe, a cone, and a ball all ask for distances and angles. If we describe a circular cylinder with x and y, square roots appear everywhere. If we describe it by radius, angle, and height, the solid becomes almost as simple as a box.
There is a price: a small coordinate box in radius and angle is not a true rectangular box in space. Its volume has a scale factor. The next section finds that factor: r\,dr\,d\theta\,dz.
